acceptedAnswer.text: Metering is how the camera measures scene brightness to suggest an exposure. Most meters assume scenes average to mid-grey, so you must place tones deliberately using exposure compensation or Manual mode.

mainEntity.name: Is metering the same as exposure?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Metering measures light; exposure is the result of your settings (aperture, shutter, ISO). You can accept the meter’s suggestion, or bias it for your subject and intent.

mainEntity.name: Which metering mode should I use?

acceptedAnswer.text:
Guidelines:

  • Matrix/Evaluative: general use; averages the whole frame.
  • Centre-weighted: favours the middle; good for portraits/static subjects near centre.
  • Spot: tiny area (≈1–5%); use for high contrast or small subjects.
  • Highlight-weighted: prioritises bright areas to avoid clipping (stage/theatre, speculars).

mainEntity.name: Why does my camera’s meter get it wrong?

acceptedAnswer.text: Meters aim for mid-grey. Bright scenes (snow/sand) are pulled too dark; dark scenes are pushed too bright. Apply exposure compensation (+EV for bright, −EV for dark) or set placement manually.

mainEntity.name: How do I meter a backlit subject?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use Spot (or Centre-weighted) on the subject’s face and lock exposure. In Matrix, add roughly +1/3 to +1 EV. Confirm with histogram and highlight warnings.

mainEntity.name: How should I meter a high-contrast landscape with bright sky?

acceptedAnswer.text: Spot a bright cloud and bias slightly down (≈ −1/3 to −2/3 EV) to hold detail. If shadows go too deep, bracket for HDR or use a graduated ND.

mainEntity.name: What EV adjustments are typical in tricky scenes?

acceptedAnswer.text:
Starting points:

  • Snow/sand: +1 to +2 EV
  • Dark subjects/backgrounds: −1 to −2 EV
  • Bright sky protection: −1/3 to −2/3 EV
  • Stage/spotlight: 0 EV with highlight-weighted, or Spot −1/3 EV
Always confirm with histogram/blinkies.

mainEntity.name: What’s the difference between Spot and Partial metering?

acceptedAnswer.text: Spot reads a very small area (≈1–5% around the focus point). Partial is a slightly larger central area (brand-dependent). Use Spot for precise placement on small subjects; Partial when the subject occupies more of the frame.
No. IS/IBIS reduces camera shake but doesn’t change the meter reading. It lets you use slower shutter speeds for static scenes, but moving subjects still need faster shutter speeds.

mainEntity.name: Should I use exposure compensation or Manual mode?

acceptedAnswer.text: Av/Tv + exposure compensation is fast when light changes. Manual (or M + Auto ISO) gives consistency for panoramas, interiors, or deliberate tone placement. Both work—choose based on how quickly light/scene changes.

mainEntity.name: What is highlight-weighted metering and when is it useful?

acceptedAnswer.text: Highlight-weighted prioritises bright regions to prevent clipping. It’s ideal for stage/theatre, backlit edges, or specular reflections where preserving highlight detail matters most.

mainEntity.name: Where can I download the Metering checklist?

acceptedAnswer.text: Get the free 1-page PDF here: Metering Field Checklist.
